title: "podman run" can write to /dev/null when in inside chroot, "podman start" however cannot
body:
**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

I make a Podman container and inside this container I create a debian image in the form of a subdirectory, to be later written to devices via rsync over the network. I create the container this way (not the --privileged) call, as root-FULL podman because of peculiarities with /dev/ device nodes otherwise.

```
sudo podman run \
	--mount type=bind,source=$(PWD)/downloads/apt.$(DEBIAN_RELEASE).$(ARCH),destination=/var/cache/apt/archives,rw \
	--mount type=bind,source=$(PWD)/../images,destination=/d/images,rw \
	--mount type=bind,source=$(PWD)/downloads/,destination=/d/mkimage/downloads,rw \
	--mount type=bind,source=$(PWD)/..,destination=/ro,ro \
	--privileged \
	--tty \
	--interactive \
	--user 1000:1000 \
	--hostname dev \
	--name $(DEBIAN_RELEASE).$(ARCH).dev \
	$(DEBIAN_RELEASE).$(ARCH).base \
	/ro/mkimage/bin/container
```

Now, the funny thing: inside this container there will be a directory (named image/) which contains a future Debian image. Complete with a /image/dev/null device. The setup process is complicated, but for now, just use a debootstrap directory for simplicity:

```
user@dev:~/mkimage$ sudo su
root@dev:/d/mkimage# mkdir image
root@dev:/d/mkimage# rsync -a image.debootstrap.bullseye.i386/ image/
root@dev:/d/mkimage# chroot image
root@dev:/# echo foo >/dev/null
root@dev:/#
```

Nice. Now, I exit the chroot, the sudo and finally the container. And the I enter the container again:

```
sudo podman start \
	--interactive \
	--attach \
	$(DEBIAN_RELEASE).$(ARCH).dev
```

Suddenly I get an error if I try to write to /dev/null inside the chroot:

```
user@dev:~/mkimage$ sudo su
root@dev:/d/mkimage# chroot image
root@dev:/# echo foo >/dev/null
bash: /dev/null: Permission denied
```

(Oh, and I don't use SELINUX, so that cannot be the culprit)

BTW, the stat of the /dev/null inside and outside of the chroot looks completely sane to me:

```
root@dev:/d/mkimage# stat image/dev/null
  File: image/dev/null
  Size: 0         	Blocks: 0          IO Block: 4096   character special file
Device: 41h/65d	Inode: 9569583     Links: 1     Device type: 1,3
Access: (0666/crw-rw-rw-)  Uid: (    0/    root)   Gid: (    0/    root)
Access: 2022-10-06 10:11:33.287188200 +0000
Modify: 2022-10-06 07:01:30.905627840 +0000
Change: 2022-10-06 10:11:33.287188200 +0000
 Birth: 2022-10-06 10:11:33.287188200 +0000
root@dev:/d/mkimage# chroot image
root@dev:/# stat /dev/null
  File: /dev/null
  Size: 0         	Blocks: 0          IO Block: 4096   character special file
Device: 41h/65d	Inode: 9569583     Links: 1     Device type: 1,3
Access: (0666/crw-rw-rw-)  Uid: (    0/    root)   Gid: (    0/    root)
Access: 2022-10-06 10:11:33.287188200 +0000
Modify: 2022-10-06 07:01:30.905627840 +0000
Change: 2022-10-06 10:11:33.287188200 +0000
 Birth: 2022-10-06 10:11:33.287188200 +0000
```

**Describe the results you expected:**

I expected "echo foo >/dev/null" inside a chroot inside a container started with "podman start" to work.

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

Maybe the "podman start" command needs a --privileged command line option?  Or maybe it should store the fact in it's metadata and re-enable it when the container is later started via "podman start" ?

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
podman --version
podman version 4.3.0-dev
```
It's self-compiled from git. Quite recent, `git describe --tags` says v4.3.0-rc1-88-g6cf34000b

**Output of `podman info`:**

```

  arch: amd64
```

**Package info (e.g. output of `rpm -q podman` or `apt list podman` or `brew info podman`):**

Not applicable, self-compiled, `git describe --tags` says v4.3.0-rc1-88-g6cf34000b

**Have you tested with the latest version of Podman and have you checked the Podman Troubleshooting Guide? (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md)**

Yes, this git version is quite new, from yesterday.

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**

Nothing, directly on the bare machine running Debian Stable (a.k.a. Bullseye) 64bit with kernel 5.18.0-0.deb11.4-amd64

comments.body:
could you access the file if you don't enter the chroot first?

Could you please show also the output of `grep ^Cap /proc/self/status` before and after entering the chroot?

Here is the Cap outside of "podman":

```
schurig@desktop:~$ grep ^Cap /proc/self/status
CapInh:	0000000000000000
CapPrm:	0000000000000000
CapEff:	0000000000000000
CapBnd:	000001ffffffffff
CapAmb:	0000000000000000
```

Here is the cap while running "podman run":

```
user@dev:~/mkimage$ grep ^Cap /proc/self/status
CapInh:	0000000000000000
CapPrm:	0000000000000000
CapEff:	0000000000000000
CapBnd:	000001ffffffffff
CapAmb:	0000000000000000
user@dev:~/mkimage$ echo foo >/dev/null 
user@dev:~/mkimage$ echo foo >image/dev/null 
user@dev:~/mkimage$ sudo su
root@dev:/d/mkimage# grep ^Cap /proc/self/status
CapInh:	0000000000000000
CapPrm:	000001ffffffffff
CapEff:	000001ffffffffff
CapBnd:	000001ffffffffff
CapAmb:	0000000000000000
root@dev:/d/mkimage# echo foo >/dev/null 
root@dev:/d/mkimage# echo foo >image/dev/null 
root@dev:/d/mkimage# chroot image
root@dev:/# echo foo >/dev/null 
```

And here is the cap while inside "docker start":

```
user@dev:~/mkimage$ grep ^Cap /proc/self/status 
CapInh:	0000000000000000
CapPrm:	0000000000000000
CapEff:	0000000000000000
CapBnd:	000001ffffffffff
CapAmb:	0000000000000000
user@dev:~/mkimage$ echo foo >/dev/null 
user@dev:~/mkimage$ echo foo >image/dev/null 
bash: image/dev/null: Permission denied
```

Here we have the first difference. So it turns out that the chroot wasn't really needed.

```
user@dev:~/mkimage$ sudo su
root@dev:/d/mkimage# grep ^Cap /proc/self/status
CapInh:	0000000000000000
CapPrm:	000001ffffffffff
CapEff:	000001ffffffffff
CapBnd:	000001ffffffffff
root@dev:/d/mkimage# echo foo >/dev/null 
root@dev:/d/mkimage# echo foo >image/dev/null
bash: image/dev/null: Permission denied
```

And for the sake of completeness (I don't think it's actually needed), here are the Caps form inside the chroot:

```
root@dev:/d/mkimage# mount -o bind /proc image/proc
root@dev:/d/mkimage# chroot image
root@dev:/# grep ^Cap /proc/self/status
CapInh:	0000000000000000
CapPrm:	000001ffffffffff
CapEff:	000001ffffffffff
CapBnd:	000001ffffffffff
CapAmb:	0000000000000000
```

Oh, and the `--privileged` command line option from the "podman run" call made it into the container description: I still suspect it is ignored when doing a "podman start" call:

```
root@desktop:/home/schurig/d/mkimage# podman container inspect bullseye.i386.dev | grep Privileged
               "Privileged": true,
```

Can you show me the 

> Here we have the first difference. So it turns out that the chroot wasn't really needed.
> 
> ```
> user@dev:~/mkimage$ sudo su
> root@dev:/d/mkimage# grep ^Cap /proc/self/status
> CapInh:	0000000000000000
> CapPrm:	000001ffffffffff
> CapEff:	000001ffffffffff
> CapBnd:	000001ffffffffff
> root@dev:/d/mkimage# echo foo >/dev/null 
> root@dev:/d/mkimage# echo foo >image/dev/null
> bash: image/dev/null: Permission denied
> ```

thanks.

Who is the owner for the `image/dev/null` file now?  Could you do a `stat /dev/null image/dev/null`?

EDIT: nevermind my last request, you've already showed it
could you then show the output of `stat -f /dev/null image/dev/null` and `cat /proc/self/mountinfo`?  I wonder if there is any nodev mount option causing that behavior
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
Since we heard no feedback, closing. Reopen if you can provide @giuseppe feedback.
